events.label: reasoning
events.text:
The scenario: backstabbed device on 20A circuit. NEC 110.14 covers electrical connections. Backstabbing (push-in termination) on a 20A circuit — is it compliant? NEC 110.14 requires connections to be tight and suitable for the conductor. Backstab connections on 20A receptacles are generally not allowed because 20A receptacles typically don't have backstab terminals rated for 20A — actually, the issue is that backstab connections are typically only rated for 14 AWG (15A circuits). On 20A circuits using 12 AWG, backstabbing is not permitted because the push-in holes are only rated for 14 AWG wire. So this would be non-compliant.

The scenario says "TRD: legal but journeymen won't" — TRD might mean "trade rule of thumb" or similar. But actually, backstabbing on 20A circuits is NOT legal because the backstab terminals are only rated for 14 AWG/15A. So it's non-compliant per NEC 110.14 and manufacturer requirements.

Let me think more carefully. Backstab connections (push-in) on receptacles are typically listed for 14 AWG only (15A circuits). Using them on a 20A circuit with 12 AWG would not be possible (12 AWG won't fit) or if somehow done, would be non-compliant. So this fails.

Decision: fail
Risk: medium (potential for loose connection, arcing, fire)
Component conditions: non-compliant, possibly installed-defective
Percent complete: rough-complete, maybe 85%
Confidence: high, 0.9
